From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Slate phone](/w/index.php?title=Slate_phone&redirect=no "Slate phone"))

Phone's size, shape and style

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/Foldable_phones.jpg/250px-Foldable_phones.jpg)](/wiki/File:Foldable_phones.jpg)

The [Samsung Galaxy Z series](/wiki/Samsung_Galaxy_Z_series "Samsung Galaxy Z series") are the foldable smartphones.

The **[form factor](/wiki/Form_factor_(design) "Form factor (design)")** of a [mobile phone](/wiki/Mobile_phone "Mobile phone") is its size, shape, and style, as well as the layout and position of its major components.

## With one non-movable section

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Form_factor_(mobile_phones)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: With one non-movable section")]

### Bar

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Form_factor_(mobile_phones)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Bar")]

A **bar** (also known as a slab, block, candybar) phone takes the shape of a [cuboid](/wiki/Cuboid "Cuboid"),[[1]](#cite_note-1) usually with rounded corners and/or edges. The name is derived from the rough resemblance to a [chocolate bar](/wiki/Chocolate_bar "Chocolate bar") in size and shape. This form factor is widely used by a variety of manufacturers, such as [Nokia](/wiki/Nokia "Nokia") and [Sony Ericsson](/wiki/List_of_Sony_Ericsson_products#Bar_phones "List of Sony Ericsson products"). Bar-type smartphones commonly have the screen and keypad on a single face. Sony had a well-known '[Mars Bar](/wiki/Mars_Bar "Mars Bar")' phone model CM-H333 in 1993 that was longer and thinner than the typical bar phone.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Bar phones without a full keyboard tend to have a 3×4 numerical keypad; text is often generated on such systems using the [Text on 9 keys](/wiki/T9_(predictive_text) "T9 (predictive text)") algorithm.

#### Keyboard bars

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Form_factor_(mobile_phones)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Keyboard bars")]

These are variants of bars that have a full [QWERTY](/wiki/QWERTY "QWERTY") keyboard on the front. While they are technically the same as a regular bar phone, the keyboard and all the buttons make them look significantly different. Devices like these were popular in the mid to late 2000s, but lost popularity afterward. The [BlackBerry](/wiki/BlackBerry "BlackBerry") line from [Research In Motion (RIM)](/wiki/BlackBerry_Limited "BlackBerry Limited") was particularly popular and influential in this category. The [Unihertz Titan](/wiki/Unihertz_Titan "Unihertz Titan") series is a series of QWERTY bar phones with modern Android software, produced in the 2020s.

### Brick

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Form_factor_(mobile_phones)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Brick")]

"**Brick**" is a slang term used to refer to large, outdated rectangular phones, typically early devices with large batteries and electronics. These early phones, such as the [Motorola DynaTAC](/wiki/Motorola_DynaTAC "Motorola DynaTAC"),[[3]](#cite_note-3) have been displaced by newer smaller models which offer greater portability thanks to smaller antennas and slimmer battery packs.

* [![A Motorola DynaTAC 8000X, a brick phone from 1984](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/DynaTAC8000X.jpg/120px-DynaTAC8000X.jpg)](/wiki/File:DynaTAC8000X.jpg "A Motorola DynaTAC 8000X, a brick phone from 1984")

  A [Motorola DynaTAC](/wiki/Motorola_DynaTAC "Motorola DynaTAC") 8000X, a brick phone from 1984
* [![A Mobira Cityman 450, a brick phone from 1985](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Mobira_Cityman_450.JPG/120px-Mobira_Cityman_450.JPG)](/wiki/File:Mobira_Cityman_450.JPG "A Mobira Cityman 450, a brick phone from 1985")

  A [Mobira Cityman 450](/w/index.php?title=Mobira_Cityman_450&action=edit&redlink=1 "Mobira Cityman 450 (page does not exist)"), a brick phone from 1985
* [![NEC P9100, a Japanese brick from 1988](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Vintage_NEC_P9100_Series_Portable_Cellular_Telephone%2C_Made_In_Japan%2C_Instruction_Manual_-_Copyright_1988_%2842814759832%29.jpg/120px-Vintage_NEC_P9100_Series_Portable_Cellular_Telephone%2C_Made_In_Japan%2C_Instruction_Manual_-_Copyright_1988_%2842814759832%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vintage_NEC_P9100_Series_Portable_Cellular_Telephone,_Made_In_Japan,_Instruction_Manual_-_Copyright_1988_(42814759832).jpg "NEC P9100, a Japanese brick from 1988")

  [NEC P9100](/w/index.php?title=NEC_P9100&action=edit&redlink=1 "NEC P9100 (page does not exist)"), a Japanese brick from 1988

However, "brick" has more recently been applied to older phone models in general, including non-bar form factors (flip, slider, swivel, etc.), and even early touchscreen phones as well, due to their size and relative lack of functionality compared to current models on the market.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

The term "brick" has also expanded beyond smartphones to include most [non-working](/wiki/Brick_(electronics) "Brick (electronics)") [consumer electronics](/wiki/Consumer_electronics "Consumer electronics"), including a [game console](/wiki/Game_console "Game console"), [router](/wiki/Router_(computing) "Router (computing)"), or other device, that, due to a serious misconfiguration, corrupted [firmware](/wiki/Firmware "Firmware"), or a [hardware](/wiki/Computer_hardware "Computer hardware") problem, can no longer function, hence, is as technologically useful as a [brick](/wiki/Brick "Brick").[[7]](#cite_note-:0-7) The term derives from the vaguely [cuboid](/wiki/Cuboid "Cuboid") shape of many electronic devices (and their [detachable power supplies](/wiki/AC_adapter "AC adapter")) and the suggestion that the device can function only as a lifeless, square object, [paperweight](/wiki/Paperweight "Paperweight") or doorstop. This term is commonly used as a verb. For example, "I bricked my [MP3 player](/wiki/Digital_audio_player "Digital audio player") when I tried to modify its [firmware](/wiki/Firmware "Firmware")." It can also be used as a noun, for example, "If it's corrupted and you apply using [fastboot](/wiki/Fastboot "Fastboot"), your device is a brick." In the common usage of the term, "bricking" suggests that the damage is so serious as to have rendered the device permanently unusable.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

### Slate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Form_factor_(mobile_phones)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Slate")]

A **slate** is a [smartphone](/wiki/Smartphone "Smartphone") form with few to no physical buttons, instead relying upon a [touchscreen](/wiki/Touchscreen "Touchscreen") and an onscreen [virtual keyboard](/wiki/Virtual_keyboard "Virtual keyboard") for input.[[9]](#cite_note-9) The first commercially available touchscreen phone was a brick phone, the [IBM Simon](/wiki/IBM_Simon "IBM Simon") Personal Communicator, released in 1994.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The success of the [iPhone](/wiki/IPhone_(1st_generation) "IPhone (1st generation)"), which was released by [Apple](/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc.") in 2007, is considered by some to be largely responsible for the influence and achievement of this design.

Some unusual "slate" designs include that of [LG New Chocolate (BL40)](/wiki/LG_New_Chocolate_(BL40) "LG New Chocolate (BL40)"), or the [Samsung Galaxy Round](/wiki/Samsung_Galaxy_Round "Samsung Galaxy Round"), which is curved.

* [![HTC Magician, a pre-contemporary slate from 2004](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2a/Qtek-S100.jpg/250px-Qtek-S100.jpg)](/wiki/File:Qtek-S100.jpg "HTC Magician, a pre-contemporary slate from 2004")

  [LG Prada](/wiki/LG_Prada "LG Prada"), a slate phone from 2006

#### Phablet

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Form_factor_(mobile_phones)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Phablet")]

Main article: [Phablet](/wiki/Phablet "Phablet")

The **phablet** is a subset of the slate/touchscreen. A [portmanteau](/wiki/Portmanteau "Portmanteau") of the words [phone](/wiki/Smartphone "Smartphone") and [tablet](/wiki/Tablet_computer "Tablet computer"), phablets are a class of mobile device designed to combine or straddle the size of a slate smartphone together with a tablet. Phablets typically have screens that measure (diagonally) greater than 5.3 inches, and are considerably larger than most high-end slate smartphones of the time (i.e. the [Samsung Galaxy Note II](/wiki/Samsung_Galaxy_Note_II "Samsung Galaxy Note II") smartlet versus the [Samsung Galaxy S III](/wiki/Samsung_Galaxy_S_III "Samsung Galaxy S III") smartphone), which have to be 5.2 inches or less to be known as a smartphone, though significantly smaller than tablets (which must be 7 inches or above to be considered as such).

#### Multi-screen

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Form_factor_(mobile_phones)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Multi-screen")]

The **multi-screen** is of basically the slate form factor, but with two touchscreens.

Some have a small separate screen above the main screens, the [LG V10](/wiki/LG_V10 "LG V10") and LG V20.

Other multi-screen form factors has screens on both sides of the phone. In the case of [Yotaphone](/wiki/Yotaphone "Yotaphone") and [Siam 7X](/wiki/Siam_7X "Siam 7X"), they have normal touchscreens on the front, but on the backside is an [e-ink screen](/wiki/E_Ink "E Ink"), which enables using the cases in a fashion similar to reading a book.

The presence of the front camera for taking selfies has been an essential feature in smartphones; however, it is difficult to achieve a bezelless screen, as was the trend in the later 2010s. The [Nubia X](/wiki/Nubia_X "Nubia X"), Nubia Z20 and [Vivo NEX Dual Display](/wiki/Vivo_NEX_Dual_Display "Vivo NEX Dual Display") have solved this, combining the use of the main camera and a smaller second rear screen, eliminating the front camera.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### Clamshell / Flip

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Form_factor_(mobile_phones)&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Clamshell / Flip")]

Main article: [Clamshell design](/wiki/Clamshell_design "Clamshell design")

A **flip** or **clamshell** phone consists of two or more sections that are connected by [hinges](/wiki/Hinge "Hinge"), allowing the phone to flip open then fold closed in order to become more compact. Clamshell came to be used as generic for this form factor. Flip phone referred to phones that opened on the vertical axis (as clamshells disappeared from the market, the terms again became disambiguated). Flip began achieving widespread popularity around 2001 and by the mid-2000s, "flip" designs reached the peak of their availability and declined afterward, being replaced by sliders which in turn were completely replaced by slate smartphones. Precedents for this formfactor can be found in designs such as the StarTrek [Communicator](/wiki/Communicator_(Star_Trek) "Communicator (Star Trek)") and the [Grillo telephone](/wiki/Grillo_telephone "Grillo telephone"), both from the mid-1960s.[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)

[Motorola](/wiki/Motorola "Motorola") was once owner of a trademark for the term *flip phone*,[[19]](#cite_note-19) but the term *flip phone* has become [genericized](/wiki/Genericized_trademark "Genericized trademark") and used more frequently than *clamshell* in colloquial speech. Motorola was the manufacturer of the famed [StarTAC](/wiki/Motorola_StarTAC "Motorola StarTAC") flip phone in the 1990s, as well as the [RAZR](/wiki/Motorola_RAZR "Motorola RAZR") in the mid-2000s. In these styles, when flipped open, the phone's screen and keyboard are available. When flipped shut, the phone becomes much smaller and more portable than when it is opened for use. On most modern flip phones, there is usually a small display on the back of the screen to indicate the time and any incoming calls/text messages whilst the phone is closed.

There were also flip "down" phones, like the [Motorola MicroTAC](/wiki/Motorola_MicroTAC "Motorola MicroTAC") series and was also widely used by [Ericsson](/wiki/Ericsson "Ericsson"). Originally these were called "flip" phones in the 1990s, while what is now known as "flip phone" used to be called "folding" or "folder" phones.

In 2010, Motorola introduced a different kind of flip phone with its [Backflip](/wiki/Motorola_Backflip "Motorola Backflip") smartphone. When closed, one side is the screen and the other is a physical QWERTY keyboard. The hinge is on a long edge of the phone instead of a short edge, and when flipped out the screen is above the keyboard. Another unusual flip form was seen on the luxury [Serene](/wiki/Serene_(phone) "Serene (phone)"), a partnership between [Samsung](/wiki/Samsung "Samsung") and [Bang & Olufsen](/wiki/Bang_%26_Olufsen "Bang & Olufsen"). The [Nokia Communicator](/wiki/Nokia_Communicator "Nokia Communicator") series is also an example of clamshell, but not in the typical "flip phone" style.

### Slider

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Form_factor_(mobile_phones)&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Slider")]

This section is an excerpt from [Slider (mobile phones)](/wiki/Slider_(mobile_phones) "Slider (mobile phones)").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Slider_(mobile_phones)&action=edit)]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/3/3d/My_phone_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:My_phone_-_panoramio.jpg)

A 'side slider' mobile phone

[Slider](/wiki/Slider_(mobile_phones) "Slider (mobile phones)") is a general term of a form factor in mobile phones composed of usually two, but sometimes more, sections that slide past each other on rails. Most slider phones have a display segment which houses the device's screen, while another segment contains the keypad or keyboard and slides out for use. The goal of a sliding form factor is to allow the operator to take advantage of full physical keyboards or keypads, without sacrificing portability, by *retracting* them into the phone when they are not in use.

Sliders supplanted the [flip](/wiki/Flip_phone "Flip phone") form, since they allowed manufacturers to pack more keypad buttons and features (especially the side slider or [QWERTY](/wiki/QWERTY "QWERTY") slider) into the same form factor, while [touchscreen](/wiki/Touchscreen "Touchscreen") interfaces were still in their infancy. By the late-2000s, "slider" designs reached the peak of their popularity and declined afterward, being completely replaced by slate form factors with well-developed touch interfaces. Sliders have also been used in non-phone devices, for example on the [Mylo](/wiki/Sony_Mylo "Sony Mylo").

#### Pop up camera

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Form_factor_(mobile_phones)&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Pop up camera")]

Phones like the [OnePlus 7 Pro](/wiki/OnePlus_7_Pro "OnePlus 7 Pro"),[[32]](#cite_note-guacamole-32) [Oppo Find X](/wiki/Oppo_Find_X "Oppo Find X")[[33]](#cite_note-33) and [Vivo Nex](/wiki/Vivo_Nex "Vivo Nex")[[34]](#cite_note-34) hide front cameras within the body of the devices in motorized pop-up modules to create a bezelless front face fully occupied by screens without any cutout while keeping a front-facing camera that can move up when required. Vivo V15 Pro [[35]](#cite_note-35) and Centric S1 [[36]](#cite_note-36) also come with a pop-up front-facing camera mechanism. One major drawback with pop-up cameras is their inherent complexity due to the mechanism used, as the components such as the motor take up space within the phone's internals which may also fail over time.[[32]](#cite_note-guacamole-32)

### Swivel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Form_factor_(mobile_phones)&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Swivel")]

A **swivel** phone is composed of multiple—usually two—segments, which swivel past each other about a sagittal [axis](/wiki/Axis_of_rotation "Axis of rotation") (most of the time). Use of the swiveling form factor has similar goals to that of the slider, but this form factor is less widely used. Samples are, LG U900 + 960 + V9000, [Motorola Flipout](/wiki/Motorola_Flipout "Motorola Flipout") + [V70](/wiki/Motorola_V70 "Motorola V70") + V80, [Nokia 7370](/wiki/Nokia_7370 "Nokia 7370"), [Siemens SK65](/wiki/Siemens_SK65 "Siemens SK65"), [Samsung Juke](/wiki/Samsung_Juke "Samsung Juke") + [Samsung SGH-X830](/w/index.php?title=Samsung_SGH-X830&action=edit&redlink=1 "Samsung SGH-X830 (page does not exist)"), and Sony Ericsson S700i + [W600](/wiki/Sony_Ericsson_W600 "Sony Ericsson W600"). The [LG Wing](/wiki/LG_Wing "LG Wing") implemented this feature into a slate design smartphone, with two touchscreens.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/SidekickII.jpg/250px-SidekickII.jpg)](/wiki/File:SidekickII.jpg)

[T-Mobile Sidekick](/wiki/T-Mobile_Sidekick "T-Mobile Sidekick") II, looks more like a slider but has a swiveling display

Other examples include the [Sierra Wireless Voq](/wiki/Sierra_Wireless_Voq "Sierra Wireless Voq") which combines a candybar with a keyboard "side-flip"; the [Nokia Communicator](/wiki/Nokia_Communicator "Nokia Communicator") series which utilise both a candybar and a clamshell with a QWERTY keyboard; the [Siemens SK65](/wiki/Siemens_SK65 "Siemens SK65") which is a swivel candybar with a QWERTY keyboard;[[37]](#cite_note-37) and the [T-Mobile Sidekick](/wiki/T-Mobile_Sidekick "T-Mobile Sidekick") where the display swivels up with a QWERTY keyboard.

Some mobile phones use more than one form, such as the [Nokia N90](/wiki/Nokia_N90 "Nokia N90"), Nokia 6260, Sharp SX862, Samsung SGH-P910, Samsung FlipShot SCH-U900, Samsung Alias series or Panasonic FOMA P900iV, which use both a swivel and a flip axis.

### Mixed

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Form_factor_(mobile_phones)&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Mixed")]

Some phones use a combination of form factors, so, [Nokia N93](/wiki/Nokia_N93 "Nokia N93"), P906i, W61SH use a combination of swivel and flip. Bezelless [Samsung Galaxy A80](/wiki/Samsung_Galaxy_A80 "Samsung Galaxy A80")[[38]](#cite_note-38) only has rear cameras that are housed in a motorized module, using a combination of slider and swivel they achieve the rotation to the front to be used for selfies.
